title: Security & DevSecOps
summary: Shift-left security, SAST/DAST, secrets management, OWASP Top 10 for APIs, and secure supply chain.

sections.content:
## Shift-Left Security

Shift-left means moving security checks earlier in the SDLC — finding vulnerabilities during development rather than in production. The later a bug is found, the more expensive it is to fix.

```
Cost to fix:
Design:   $1
Dev:      $10
Test:     $100
Prod:     $10,000+
```

sections.title: DevSecOps Pipeline
sections.content:
graph LR
    Code[Code] -->|SAST\nSonarQube, SpotBugs| Commit[Commit]
    Commit -->|Secret scanning\nGitGuardian, truffleHog| Build[Build]
    Build -->|Dependency scan\nOWASP Dependency-Check| Test[Test]
    Test -->|Container scan\nTrivy, Snyk| Image[Container Image]
    Image -->|DAST\nOWASP ZAP| Deploy[Deploy]
    Deploy -->|Runtime protection\nFalco, WAF| Prod[Production]
    style Code fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style Prod fill:#059669,color:#fff

## OWASP API Security Top 10

| # | Vulnerability | Prevention |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Broken Object Level Authorization** | Validate user owns resource before every access |
| 2 | **Broken Authentication** | Strong JWT validation, proper session management |
| 3 | **Broken Object Property Level Auth** | Filter what fields users can read/write |
| 4 | **Unrestricted Resource Consumption** | Rate limiting, request size limits |
| 5 | **Broken Function Level Authorization** | Check permissions, not just authentication |
| 6 | **Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Flows** | Rate limit sensitive endpoints (login, forgot password) |
| 7 | **Server Side Request Forgery (SSRF)** | Validate/allowlist URLs in user input |
| 8 | **Security Misconfiguration** | Disable debug, restrict CORS, set security headers |
| 9 | **Improper Inventory Management** | Document and retire old API versions |
| 10 | **Unsafe Consumption of APIs** | Validate and sanitize data from external APIs |

## Secrets Management

Never hardcode secrets. Never commit them to Git.

### Hierarchy of secrets management:
1. **Environment variables** — basic, works everywhere
2. **Kubernetes Secrets** — base64 encoded, better with encryption at rest
3. **Vault (HashiCorp)** — dynamic secrets, audit log, auto-rotation
4. **Cloud-native** — AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key Vault

### Spring + Vault
```yaml
# application.yml
spring:
  cloud:
    vault:
      host: vault.internal
      port: 8200
      authentication: KUBERNETES  # use K8s service account token
      kv:
        enabled: true
        backend: secret
      # Vault path: secret/order-service/production

# Result: vault secret auto-injected as Spring properties
# ${db.password} comes from Vault, not application.properties
```

### Secret Scanning in CI
```yaml
# GitHub Actions
- name: Scan for secrets
  uses: trufflesecurity/trufflehog@main
  with:
    path: ./
    base: main
    extra_args: --only-verified
```

## Security Headers

```java
@Configuration
public class SecurityHeadersConfig {
    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain headers(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        return http
            .headers(headers -> headers
                .contentSecurityPolicy(csp -> csp
                    .policyDirectives("default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'"))
                .frameOptions(frame -> frame.deny())         // Prevent clickjacking
                .xssProtection(xss -> xss.enable())         // XSS protection
                .referrerPolicy(rp -> rp                    // Limit referrer
                    .policy(STRICT_ORIGIN_WHEN_CROSS_ORIGIN))
            )
            .build();
    }
}
```

### Essential HTTP Security Headers:
| Header | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Content-Security-Policy` | Prevent XSS by allowlisting resource origins |
| `X-Frame-Options: DENY` | Prevent clickjacking |
| `Strict-Transport-Security` | Force HTTPS |
| `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` | Prevent MIME sniffing |
| `Permissions-Policy` | Restrict browser features |

sections.question: What is Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) and why is it the #1 API vulnerability?
options: When API authentication tokens are broken or expired
When an API fails to verify that the requesting user actually owns the object they're trying to access by ID
When API objects are not encrypted in transit
When API response objects contain too much sensitive data
sections.explanation: BOLA (also known as IDOR — Insecure Direct Object Reference) happens when you return or modify a resource by ID without checking the requester owns it. An attacker simply changes the ID in the request (order/123 → order/456) to access another user's data. It's #1 because APIs naturally expose IDs and the check is easy to forget.
